message:
docs: cod-fs/cc-fs session close — saturation confirmed, final report landed

UPGRADE_LOG.md extends the 2026-04-22 full-review entry with the cod-fs
and cc-fs wrap-up sections:

- Repeated `rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_frankenfs_cod
  cargo test --workspace --all-targets` passes consistently surface
  exactly the same 4 ffs-harness conformance failures tracked by
  bd-uyjcz, bd-efc2j, bd-r8c8r, and bd-ew490 — no new failure class
  surfaced, confirming the review frontier is saturated.
- cod-fs final report: 36 beads reconciled (parity coverage, stale-tracker
  cleanup, fuzz-harness implementation, blocked syscall-boundary
  closeouts) and 17+ review/fuzz follow-up beads filed for remaining
  workspace gate failures, conformance regressions, and ffs-core /
  ffs-fuse fuzz targets.
- Dependency pin: asupersync is consistently held at 0.3.1 across the
  workspace, ffs-* crates, and Cargo.lock.
- cc-fs final verification table: clippy PASS (6 vendored-fuser warnings
  only), cargo test 4 pre-existing failures (bd-uyjcz), fuzz audit
  saturated after 5+ passes, 5 bugs fixed, 17 fuzz beads filed, 19/19
  workspace crates audited.
- Both agents report DONE/IDLE pending new code-surface changes or
  prioritization of the remaining open review beads.

.beads/issues.jsonl records the bead-status churn behind those reports:
eight beads touched (bd-3g31b, bd-7xfny, bd-cb0kd, bd-dn1hf, bd-h9lpq,
bd-orbjt, bd-s35v4, bd-ymg8v) across closed/in_progress/open transitions
that the session narrative already describes at a high level.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-7tn4","title":"PoR for cryptographic durability audit (Graveyard Entry 11.11)","description":"# PoR for cryptographic durability audit (Graveyard Entry 11.11)

## GOAL
Implement Proofs of Retrievability (PoR) for cryptographic verification that stored data is intact and retrievable, without reading the entire dataset.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 11.11: PoR (Juels-Kaliski 2007, Shacham-Waters 2008) allows a verifier to challenge a storage system with random queries. The storage system responds with proofs that specific blocks exist and are correct. If the proofs verify, the entire dataset is retrievable with high probability.

## DESIGN
- Challenge: verifier sends random block indices + random coefficients
- Response: storage computes linear combination of challenged blocks weighted by coefficients
- Verification: verifier checks response against precomputed authenticators
- Authenticators: computed once at file creation, stored alongside data

## APPLICABILITY
- Future feature: useful for cloud/remote storage verification
- Complementary to RaptorQ: PoR proves data exists, RaptorQ repairs it if it doesn't
- Useful for auditing: periodic PoR challenges prove filesystem integrity without full scan

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] PoR authenticator generation at file creation
- [ ] Challenge-response protocol implemented
- [ ] Verification: false positive rate < 2^-128
- [ ] Benchmark: challenge/response time for 1GB file","acceptance_criteria":"PoR authenticator generation at file creation time. Challenge-response protocol implemented with configurable challenge count. Verification: false positive rate below 2^-128. Authenticators stored alongside data in reserved metadata area. Benchmark: challenge/response time for 1GB file. Benchmark: authenticator generation time per MB. Unit tests: generate authenticators, challenge, verify for small files. Unit tests: corrupted block causes verification failure. Unit tests: uncorrupted file passes all challenges. -{"id":"bd-cb0kd","title":"[HIGH] build_btrfs_readdir_image still computes invalid tree_block_csum after explicit stamping","description":"# Review finding

The current `build_btrfs_readdir_image()` fixture already writes explicit CRC32C values into both synthetic tree blocks, but `rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_frankenfs_cod cargo test --workspace --all-targets` still fails the six btrfs directory-walk tests with `Format("invalid field: tree_block_csum (CRC32C checksum mismatch)")`.

## Evidence
- Builder already stamps both blocks: `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:23225-23378`.
- It computes `csum_root` and `csum_fs` over `image[tree + 0x20 .. tree + nodesize]` and writes them to bytes `0..4`.
- After `br-bd-o5114 repair btrfs default-root smoke fixture`, the remaining red frontier is exactly:
  - `walk_btrfs_dir_entry_items_includes_dir_index_items`
  - `btrfs_readdir_mixed_types`
  - `btrfs_readdir_empty_directory_returns_dot_dotdot`
  - `btrfs_readdir_sorted_by_index`
  - `btrfs_readdir_offset_pagination`
  - `btrfs_readdir_special_characters_in_names`
- All six still fail on the same checksum mismatch, so the root cause is no longer "missing stamp calls".

## Why this is high severity
This keeps the full workspace test gate red and shows the older broad review bead is now under-specified. The next implementation pass needs to debug the actual checksum semantics, not just add stamp writes.

## Expected fix direction
Audit the exact checksum window, node header fields, and final byte mutations for the 16 KiB synthetic readdir fixture. Verify the bytes being hashed match what the parser later validates.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:12:53Z","created_by":"cod-fs","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:12:53Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btrfs","ffs-core","fixtures","qa","review","workspace-gate"]}

 {"id":"bd-cebm","title":"Implement FUSE fsync/flush with durability guarantees","description":"# Implement FUSE fsync/flush with durability guarantees

## GOAL
Implement correct fsync and flush semantics for FUSE mount, ensuring durability guarantees match user expectations.

## BACKGROUND
fsync is the primary durability contract:
- `fsync(fd)` must ensure all data written to fd is on stable storage
- `fdatasync(fd)` same but may skip non-essential metadata
- `flush` is called on close (cleanup, not durability)

## CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. fsync Semantics
```rust
impl FuseBackend {
    fn fsync(&self, ino: u64, fh: u64, datasync: bool, reply: fuser::ReplyEmpty) {
        // 1. Get inode from fh
        // 2. Commit any pending MVCC transaction for this file
        // 3. Flush dirty cache entries for this inode's blocks
        // 4. If !datasync: also flush inode metadata block
        // 5. Wait for I/O completion
        // 6. If repair_eager: refresh RaptorQ symbols for dirty groups
        // 7. reply.ok() or reply.error(errno)
    }
}
```

### 2. Flush Semantics
```rust
impl FuseBackend {
    fn flush(&self, ino: u64, fh: u64, lock_owner: u64, reply: fuser::ReplyEmpty) {
        // Called on close - release resources, no durability guarantee
        // 1. Release file handle
        // 2. Decrement open count
        // 3. If last close + unlinked: queue for deletion
        // 4. reply.ok()
    }
}
```

### 3. fsyncdir Semantics
```rust
impl FuseBackend {
    fn fsyncdir(&self, ino: u64, fh: u64, datasync: bool, reply: fuser::ReplyEmpty) {
        // Same as fsync but for directories
        // Ensures directory entries are durable
    }
}
```

### 4. Integration Points
- **MVCC**: fsync triggers transaction commit
- **ARC Cache**: fsync flushes dirty entries for inode
- **RaptorQ**: fsync may trigger eager symbol refresh
- **Journal**: fsync ensures journal commit block written

### 5. Error Handling
- I/O error during fsync -> EROFS or EIO
- MVCC conflict -> EAGAIN (retry)
- Timeout -> EIO with detailed logging

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: fsync flushes dirty blocks for inode
2. Unit: fdatasync skips metadata
3. Integration: fsync + crash + recovery -> data durable
4. Integration: fsync on RO mount -> EROFS
5. E2E: Write + fsync + unmount + remount -> data present

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- fsync start (debug): inode, datasync flag
- fsync cache flush (trace): block count, dirty bytes
- fsync commit (trace): commit_seq
- fsync complete (debug): duration_us, blocks_flushed
- fsync error (warn): inode, error, context

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. fsync guarantees data is on stable storage
2. fsync failure returns correct errno
3. No data loss if process crashes after successful fsync
4. Performance: fsync of single small file < 100ms","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"GentleElk","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:15:10.359139338Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-17T20:58:39.214188659Z","closed_at":"2026-02-17T20:58:39.214168702Z","close_reason":"Verified fsync/flush/fsyncdir durability semantics and focused rch test evidence; criteria satisfied","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core","durability","fuse"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-cebm","depends_on_id":"bd-20p","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:15:18.909563807Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-cebm","depends_on_id":"bd-2s4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:56:37.663794504Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":138,"issue_id":"bd-cebm","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).
